title: Aid Accountability Act of 2025
plain_summary: The Aid Accountability Act of 2025 creates tough rules for people who work for the government or get money from the government to help other countries. If these people break the rules about foreign aid, they will lose their jobs and never be allowed to work for the government again. They also have to pay back any money they used wrong. The Secretary of State will decide when someone broke the rules and will tell Congress what happened so it does not happen again.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Aid Accountability Act of twenty twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Penalties for Violating Section 104f of the Foreign Assistance Act of 1961
sections.text: This section adds new punishment rules to an existing law about foreign aid. If a federal government worker knowingly breaks the rules in section one hundred four f of the Foreign Assistance Act, three things will happen to them. First, they will be fired from their government job. Second, they can never work for the federal government again. Third, they must pay back to the government the same amount of money that was wrongly spent because of their rule breaking. If someone who gets government money like a grantee, subgrantee, contractor or other person who receives federal funds breaks these same rules, they can never receive federal money again. The Secretary of State will decide if someone broke the rules and what punishment they should get. No one can change or reverse the Secretary's decision except a federal court that has the legal power to do so. Within sixty days of making this decision, the Secretary of State must send a report to Congress. This report must explain what rule was broken, who broke it or was involved in breaking it, and what will be done to make sure it does not happen again. Each decision made by the Secretary of State must follow the process described in chapter eight of title five of the United States Code, which is commonly called the Congressional Review Act.
